data.title: ClearBridge Appreciation Strategy Q4 2024 Commentary
data.description:
By Michael Kagan and Stephen Rigo, CFA

Broad Bullishness Creates More Risk, Not Less - 4Q 2024

Market Overview

A powerful post-election rally propelled the S&P 500 Index up 2.4% in the fourth quarter, the S&P 500’s fifth consecutive quarterly advance (and eighth over the past nine quarters). The index’s 25% total return for calendar 2024 marked back-to-back years of at least 25% returns, an ascent only achieved four times since 1929 (1936, 1955, 1998, and today). At this point, we’re running out of superlatives to describe the market’s relentless advance over the past two years.

Consumer discretionary stocks led the advance in the quarter (+14.3%), the only sector to post a double-digit quarterly return. This was almost exclusively driven by Tesla (TSLA), which rose 52% as investors chased the open-ended optionality of an Elon Musk/Donald Trump alliance. Travel and consumer stocks (including Amazon (AMZN)) also fared well with investors optimistic that robust consumer spending would continue beyond the holiday shopping season. Communication services and information technology (IT) also outperformed on continued optimism around artificial intelligence. Finally, financials performed well as investors looked forward to a lighter regulatory touch for banks and credit card issuers under a Republican administration.

"Investor expectations for lower interest rates remain too aggressive and could act as a ceiling to materially higher stock market returns."

Materials and health care stocks were the worst-performing sectors in the quarter, declining -12.4% and -10.3%, respectively. Metals, mining and chemicals stocks lagged as the industrial economy remains sluggish and investors feared stubbornly high mortgage rates would keep a lid on real estate activity. Within health care, large managed care companies trailed as investors fretted about the industry’s claims-paying practices following the tragic murder of Brian Thompson, a UnitedHealth executive. Drug stocks were weak as anemic current growth collied with worries about the potential impact RFK Jr. could have on the industry should he be appointed as head of Health and Human Services.

Liquidity, the primary driver of bull and bear markets in our view, remains plentiful, with credit spreads at 20-year lows suggesting little fear of a corporate credit cycle. Bank loan officers — as measured by the Senior Loan Officer Opinion Survey (SLOOS) — are normalizing underwriting criteria following the largest non-recessionary tightening in credit standards in survey history, a tailwind for credit availability (Exhibit 1). Finally, expectations for capital markets activity (IPOs, debt issuance, M&A) are high as investors expect the Trump administration and Republican majority to lessen scrutiny on corporate deal making. However, it is not all blue skies as we start 2025. A year-end market swoon brought a spike in volatility sending nearly 80% of S&P 500 stocks below their 50-day moving average.

Exhibit 1: Credit Availability Is Increasing

As of December 31, 2024. Source: ClearBridge Investments, Barclays Research and Federal Reserve.

Outlook

Although conditions today remain conducive for risk assets, we see high expectations and sticky inflation as risks to the 2025 outlook. As evidenced by the post-election rally, markets expect Trump’s policies will unleash a capital investment cycle while technological innovation will drive a rise in productivity. However, inflation remains stubbornly close to 3%, versus the Fed’s 2% target, while wage inflation remains above 4%. A data-dependent Fed is likely done reducing interest rates given relatively easy current financial conditions and GDP growth trending around 2.5%.

Interest rates are a key medium-term risk, in our view. Investor expectations for lower interest rates remain too aggressive and could act as a ceiling to materially higher stock market returns. We believe that real interest rates in the future are more likely to be in line with longer-term historical levels, not the post–Global Financial Crisis era to which many equity investors are anchored (especially considering the likelihood for higher deficit spending). While equity markets have been willing to absorb higher discount rates to date, the gradual acceptance of a “back to the future” rate regime, as well as the indeterminable effect of future government policy actions, will likely play a greater role in equity returns in 2025.

Expectations of the impact generative AI will have on capital investment and economic growth remain sky high. Agentic AI, a type of artificial intelligence that can make decisions autonomously with limited human supervision, has replaced copilots as the buzzword in Silicon Valley. However, despite the changing terminology we see this as a “copilot 2.0” efficiency tool more akin to table stakes for software services than a step function monetization opportunity. With America’s largest technology companies committed to AI infrastructure capex, we remain focused on investor willingness to fund this massive outlay in the absence of a tangible returns on investments. With power costs and compute intensity increasing, the investment required to chase artificial general intelligence (AGI), the ability of a machine to understand or learn any intellectual task that a human being can, continues to march higher. In our view, we remain many iterations away from having humanoid robots doing our cooking or cleaning or fully autonomous driving.

Despite robust GDP growth, there remain many anemic areas in our economy, a dynamic we see as difficult to improve should rates remain at current levels. Housing remains unaffordable (Exhibit 2) and with mortgage rates sticky at ~7% (Exhibit 3); it is hard to see activity pickup without price concessions or better financing rates (or both). Manufacturing remains in a slump with the ISM Manufacturing PMI highlighting contracting activity in 25 of the past 26 months. Forward-looking demand indicators continue to suggest sluggish activity. Finally, despite currently robust consumer spending and near-full employment, the rise in credit card delinquency rates should be monitored for consumer stress at the lower-end of the income curve.

Exhibit 2: Home Affordability Is Low

As of December 31, 2024. Source: ClearBridge Investments, National Association of Realtors, Bloomberg Finance.

Exhibit 3: And Mortgage Rates Are High

As of December 31, 2024. Source: ClearBridge Investments, Bankrate.com, Bloomberg Finance.

In sum, we believe that the economy will continue its expansion in 2025 driven by incremental fiscal support, productivity gains and stable employment trends, with wage growth sustaining consumer spending. However, we feel the effect on economic growth that potential policy changes floated by the Trump administration will have — be they tax extensions, tariffs, immigration or a change in government size — are largely factored into today’s market valuations. We do expect reduced regulatory scrutiny to unlock investment and drive an M&A upcycle, which would be positive for capital markets, all else equal.

Shorter term, we worry that current expectations have become too complacent or, said more plainly, too bullish. While we agree the impact of generative AI is likely to create game-changing technologies and opportunities for investors over the next decade, we feel that this cohort of companies, which have been market leaders and where expectations are sky high, could face some volatility while digesting recent gains. They also face risks from the reality of non-linear adoption rates for new technologies, changing business models and unanticipated bottlenecks (particularly power availability and cost).

Our longer-term worry relates to the sustainability of government debt and the burden of debt service on the budget deficit. At some point the U.S. will need to increase taxes or cut spending to prevent debt costs from spiraling out of control. Deficit spending at current levels cannot last forever. Current presidential plans offer an impractical budget that would greatly increase the deficit, an outlook that worries us further. Should investors require a greater risk premium (yield) to own a risk-free Treasury, the next administration and Congress will face stark choices not in anyone’s current playbook. By no means is the U.S. dollars status as reserve currency our birthright.

Conclusion

Our expectation is that 2025 will be a more subdued, but positive, year in terms of overall equity returns, but we admit this is not a bold prediction given the excess returns of the past two years and the currently benign backdrop. Overall longer-term fundamentals remain on a solid footing. We expect small and mid cap companies, as well as more cyclical businesses, to benefit from continued growth of the U.S. economy. We believe the current backdrop has heightened the risk to the investment landscape should there be any stall in the disinflationary trend, disappointment in AI-driven capex, or government policy hiccups. We believe when everyone is bullish there is more risk to markets, not less.

We are long-term investors focused on the risk-adjusted returns a diversified portfolio can deliver through a market cycle. Rather than trying to bet on near-term earnings trends or government policy speculation, we believe it is better to look out two to three years and make investment decisions based upon our assessment of a company’s longer-term, sustainable growth rate relative to what is implied in today’s share price.

Portfolio Highlights

The ClearBridge Appreciation Strategy modestly underperformed the benchmark S&P 500 Index in the fourth quarter. On an absolute basis, the Strategy had positive contributions from seven of 11 sectors. The IT and financials sectors were the main positive contributors to performance, while health care was the main detractor.

In relative terms, an overweight to materials and an underweight to consumer discretionary caused negative sector allocation effects to outweigh positive stock selection, which was driven by strong selection in the industrials, energy and materials sectors. Selection in health care detracted.

On an individual stock basis, the biggest contributors to absolute performance during the quarter were Amazon.com, Broadcom (AVGO), Nvidia (NVDA), Apple (AAPL) and Visa (V). The biggest detractors were Eli Lilly (LLY), American Tower (AMT), Thermo Fisher Scientific (TMO), ASML (ASML) and Adobe (ADBE).

During the quarter, we initiated new positions in BJ’s Wholesale Club in consumer staples and Starbucks in consumer discretionary, and we exited Nestle in consumer staples, PPG Industries in materials and ICON in health care.

Michael Kagan, Managing Director, Portfolio Manager

Stephen Rigo, CFA, Managing Director, Portfolio Manager

Read Seeking Alpha's event policy here

This video's transcript was generated by a third party. It is not curated or reviewed and is provided for convenience and information purposes only. The accuracy and completeness of the transcript are not guaranteed.

Daniel Snyder: Hey. Welcome back to Top Stocks for 2025 here on Seeking Alpha. I'm Daniel Snyder. Thank you so much for taking the time to spend these last few sessions with us today. It has been tremendous, hasn't it? Well, we've got the best for last, and I am so thankful that you're here. But before we jump right in, let's go ahead and get a quick legal disclaimer out of the way. So if you'll stand by with me, let's just go ahead and clean this up.

Seeking Alpha does not take account of your objectives or your financial situation and does not offer any personalized investment advice. Seeking Alpha is not a licensed securities dealer, broker, US investment adviser, or investment bank.

And with that out of the way, obviously, we're about to dive into the session with Steve Cress himself, but, I mean, today has been tremendous. We've talked about PAX with Samuel Smith. We talked about PSQ with Alex King. We went through the crude tankers. We went through all of the shipping with J Mintzmyer, and now we are finally here. So Top 10 stocks. Steven Cress outperformed the markets last two years, and now we're going to be joined here by himself. But before we do that, I want go ahead and make sure we throw-up our poll for this session.

So which industry in the industrial sector do you think will perform best in 2025? You can either choose transportation, construction and engineering, aerospace and defense, or agriculture and farm machinery, and we'll get the results of those here towards the end of the session. And now with all of that out of the way, I want to introduce the Quant Titan himself, Steven Cress. Steve, thank you so much for the time joining us today.

Steven Cress: Daniel, thank you so much for having me on this incredible forum. What a lineup we had today and some just great insight and recommendations from our leaders. But, again, really, thank you for having me, and thank you to everyone who's attending and watching.

DS: Yeah. We have people viewing from all over the world right now. I mean, it really is an international market moving event. I've been watching it all day. But, Steve, we've been doing this a long time, but not everybody knows who you are. So maybe you can just give a little bit of your background for us, how you got into finance, and how you got to where you are today.

SC: Sure, Daniel. I'd be happy to. Well, I pretty much have been in finance since I graduated from college. I've had, over 35 years of experience working on Wall Street and in the city of London. The majority of my career was spent at Morgan Stanley. I worked there for 13 years where I ran a Quant prop trading desk. I also worked at Northern Trust, which was the 8th largest asset management firm in the world. And Daniel, I actually also had my own hedge fund. And, of course, I started a Fintech company that was acquired by Seeking Alpha, which has brought me here today, and it's just been a great journey. It's been a lot of fun. And, we put together a great Quant platform for our customers, and it makes it super easy to pick stocks. And we're going to show people today how I pick those stocks. And I've had a pretty good performance, a good track record over the last couple of years, and I want to show everybody how to do it.

DS: Let's dive into it. I know there's a lot in this conversation of this presentation. You're going to go over overviews and get into the picks, so let's hit it.

SC: Yeah. So today, the presentation's going to cover the state of the financial markets, a little bit of what happened in 2024, and what we can expect going into 2025. I'm also going to explain why we like Quant and our data driven process to select stocks. Of course, we're going to go over our top 10 stocks for 2025, and we're also going to briefly discuss past January of 2025, how we can pick stocks going forward and helping our subscribers really focus on the best Quant Strong Buys.

So I'm going to touch base right now on 2024. Obviously, it's a market that I think, really did far better than most people expected early on in the year. Of course, the S&P 500 surged about 23%, and NASDAQ was up 30%, and the Dow Jones was even double-digits 13%. Markets were largely driven by interest rate cuts, moderating inflation, strong corporate earnings, a return to political certainty. Obviously, it was an election year. And, finally after November, we had some certainty on the political front. We also had a resilient consumer spending and, of course, we had the AI frenzy. All those characteristics helped the market build up performance over the course of 2024, and there was a fairly strong economy to support that.

So we experienced moderate economic growth, and that was on the back of central banks worldwide easing their monetary policy. We had inflation stabilize throughout the world, and supply chains finally returned to somewhat of a normal status after the pandemic, which of course, was quite an abnormal period. And most of those factors, really laid the foundation for a solid economy in 2024.

Of course, on the interest rate front, in the back half of the year, the Fed finally cut interest rates, so we had a total of three cuts, which took it down a total of 1%. Going to 2025, we expect a few more cuts, and the hope is that, the Fed funds will fall to somewhere between a range of 3% to 4%. So most of the year was you know, it was pretty volatile, but we had some really strong runs. However, when we got at the end of the year, December actually took a bit of a dip. And for some sectors, it was more than a dip. It was almost a full-fledged correction.

So a little bit of a dark turn on the back end of the year. And as you can see on the table that we have on the left hand side, the best performing sector of the month, was consumer discretionary, and that was still in the red with technology coming in second in the red as well. And then we have basic materials and real estate get completely crushed. Basic materials were down more than 12%, and the real estate sector was down 9%. So I think it's, you know, when we look at December alone and that rotation, you know, really occurred, I would call that rotation a flight to safety.

So on a market cap weighted basis, the S&P 500 was down 2.73% in December. But on an equal weighted basis, the S&P was down a whopping 6.60%. Now, notably, we do know September and December over the last decade have typically been the worst performing months for the market. And this December, obviously, at the end of the year, wasn't any different. It was a pretty bad month. As I mentioned, all sectors went into the red, but with all sectors going into the red and as I said, we saw a flight to safety. And, really, historically, a flight to safety would have been a move to utilities or to consumer staples, but in the modern era over the last several years, a flight to safety is usually to the Magnificent 7. And the Magnificent 7 on average was up 5.48%.

So as we rotated into January, we saw, you know, tick up, and then we saw a tick down. Really not decisive yet. And I think it's kind of you know, January could be an indication of what we might see for the rest of the year. I call it sort of cloudy skies mixed with sun. So the 2025 market and economic consensus expectations, this isn't necessarily my expectations, so I'm drawing on consensus here. Most pundits expect AI and that frenzy to continue, and that will pull and push many AI stocks and secondary stocks in both directions based on what market sentiment is towards AI at that time. So we'll see some pretty wild swings in AI stocks.

Inflation could remain a little bit stubborn. Of course, you know, the central banks worldwide have felt inflation has come off a little bit, but inflation, it's always kind of sticky. It never comes down as fast as you want, and I think many people expect that over the course of 2025 as well. And on the back of that, of course, will be uncertainty with interest rates. So most, bond traders and, interest rate traders are looking for two additional cuts in 2025, but they're not expecting at any term near since we've just come off several cuts at the back half of 2024.

Recession risk for the most part, people do not expect a recession. However, we did have a tight monetary policy for quite a while, and not everybody believes that it could be smooth sailing. So there are going to be concerns as economic data points come out. On occasion, those data points may not be what the market wants, and we could see recession fears pop back up. But for the most part, nobody's expecting a hard recession, and the hope is, you know, we'll have, a continued level of normalcy and not even a soft recession.

Corporate earnings, there could be pressure on corporate profit margins, which could come from rising input costs and potentially slower consumer demand. And notably, when you look at the Magnificent 7 stocks, the forward growth rates, by most analysts will actually be lower than the year-over-year growth rates that we just saw. So on a whole, people are looking for slower growth out of the Magnificent 7 stocks.

Of course, geopolitical tensions could continue well into 2025. And then on the political front in the US, there is the prospect that there could be tariffs, which might be facilitated by the Trump Administration. Of course, at this point, it could just be jawboning, but in the course of 2025, we could see an outline for some severe tariffs or for low tariffs. We really don't know which way it's going to go. That creates uncertainty. And I think, outside of what we typically see in December with some profit taking and selling for tax reasons, I think there is uncertainty going to what will happen with the tariffs in 2025, and that's having a bit of an impact on the markets.

Of course, on a positive side, there could be an extension of the Trump tax cuts, and the market would react very positive to that. And that could actually wash out any concerns that the market has with tariffs.

So I will also highlight there are two things, historically that have led to positive returns for the market. One being that the stock market tends to perform well after the Federal Reserve cuts rates. So we had three rate cuts in 2024. That is typically very positive. So, historically, we have seen the following year the market is up.

I will also highlight that following an election year in the US, we typically see the market produce positive returns as well. So there are two catalysts there that really support positive returns for 2025, and I think that's what we can expect on the sunny side.

Here's a chart that I wanted to exhibit. This was sort of a go to chart, prior to the election. I really do want to highlight to people that in a presidential election year, typically, the returns that you would see would be lower than non-presidential election years. So I wanted to sort of just put that reminder out, and that does serve as a positive going into 2025.

And I also wanted to highlight when the Fed cuts rates, markets move higher. And on this table, you could see looking at a week following the first rate cut to a month, quarter, and then the year, you could see returns on average are very positive for the market after those rate cuts. So I'd say between the election and the Fed rate cuts, that really should work in the favor of the market even with the uncertainty that's out there.

So speaking of, rate cuts, I know the market and investors and bond traders are looking 21 days from now when the FOMC meets next. And I like this table a lot. It's updated on a daily basis. It comes out from the Fed, and you could see by looking at this that 93% of interest rate traders actually expect no cut to occur when the Fed meets next, and only 6.9% expect a 25 basis point cut. So by and large, most interest rate traders are voting that there will be no cut in the upcoming meeting.

So, Daniel, right now, I want to sort of highlight our Top 10 stocks and our Quant Vision. Of course, our Quant Vision is to eliminate emotional investing and uncover top stocks using data driven tactics. So, what I'm going to do is, sort of show you a quick slide. Actually, this is not a slide. I want to go right to our platform, Daniel. And -- on our platform, I want to show investors how if you are on the Seeking Alpha platform, you could actually go to our screener, And, I'm going to scroll down here on the screener, and you're going to be able to see there's an area. So for those who are new to the Seeking Alpha platform, you can actually click on Stock Screener, and I'm going to do that right now.

And on the Stock Screener, you can create your own screens. And if you're not in the practice of creating your own screens, we actually have pre-baked screens for individuals to just select. So when I go over by, Stocks by Quant, all I have to do is click on this right here and it's going to bring up Top Stocks by Quant rating. So it's a really great tool, this screener, and this is extremely useful when I pick stocks.

Now I will highlight, Daniel, the stocks that I'm going to pick today are not directly in the descending order that you're going to see. So part of the beauty of our platform is you could click on advanced filters, and when the advanced filters opens up, you'll see there's all these different areas that you could select from Quant factor grades to dividend grades or earnings to valuation, and you can actually formulate your own screen and add additional parameters and criteria when you want to look for stocks. So you could look on strong growth or you could look at stocks that are extremely cheap or you could look at stocks that are very profitable and you could add your own criteria to it. And that's sort of how we pick the top stocks right here.

So, from a Quant --.

DS: Steve, I do want to take a second to mention the Quant system that somebody joining right now may not be familiar with is the proprietary Seeking Alpha Quant system that updates every single day based off of, what, thousands and thousands of metrics.

SC: Daniel, that's a perfect segue into this slide right here. So, when we're looking at our Quant system, Daniel, there's a couple factors that go into it. One, we do have an overall strategy that we use, which is somewhat proprietary to Seeking Alpha, but it's basically a style that we utilize, which I often refer to as GARP, which means Growth At A Reasonable Price. So this, in my opinion and in opinion of many Quants, is a time tested strategy. Now there are Quants that will strictly have a momentum bias or value bias or growth bias. I like to have diversification in my Quant model, so that's why we go with the GARP approach. And in addition to the growth at a reasonable price, we do have additional metrics that we factor in, which will be, EPS revisions that we look at. We also look at momentum, and we also factor risk in. So there are a number of factors that go into our strategy, Daniel.

Primarily, the investment characteristics that we look at are stocks with strong fundamentals. So when I look at a stock, I want to make sure it's collectively strong on growth, value, profitability, EPS revisions, and momentum. And as I mentioned, that's sort of the strategy that we utilize. So when we look at stocks, we're actually looking at the those metrics relative to all other stocks in the sector. So by example, we're looking at profitability or earnings growth on Apple. When we review it, we're actually looking at that profitability or growth compared to the entire sector. Hence, it gives us the ability to rank a stock compared to the overall sector. And that's where our powerful computer processing comes in. And that part is very much proprietary to Seeking Alpha. We have an algorithm which uploads those investment characteristics, and we actually look at hundreds of investment characteristics that fall within those styles of value, growth, earnings, and profitability.

And our Quant system here, on a daily basis, as Daniel has mentioned, is refreshed. So we go through millions and millions of data points on a daily basis. We refresh that data, and we look at that data for each stock compared to the sector. And that's how our Quant system works, and it gives us the ability to rank from 1 to 4,500 the stocks that are in our Quant universe.

And you know what, Daniel? It's worked pretty well. So we have a great track record, and I want to demonstrate that track record. And we have a track record on a number of different levels. So if you looked at our Quant system, we are very, very transparent. So with the overall Quant system, we measure our Strong Buy stocks, and we upload it to a portfolio every single day. So on any given day, there could be anywhere from 350 to 400 Strong Buys. And if those stocks fall-off the Strong Buy, it comes out of this Strong Buy portfolio. So there's no cherry picking here. We look at every single stock. And if you go back to 2010, Daniel, you could see that we've had incredible performance. So if you look on the right hand side, if you had invested $10,000 in 2010, buying those Strong Buys, your return would be $306,000. By comparison, if you put that $10,000 into the S&P 500, the return would be $59,000. So that is a huge return on an annualized basis. It's a return of about 26%. And as I mentioned, we want to be really transparent.

Now when you look at this process, it's not really an investable process. Most individuals are not going to be able to rebalance 400 stocks every single day. So the reason why I actually highlight this slide is to demonstrate the strength of this strategy. And it's really that strategy that lends stocks to become Strong Buys, focusing on stocks that are collectively strong on value, growth, earnings, EPS revisions, and momentum. And by highlighting the stocks with those strong attributes, it becomes a Strong Buy, and it demonstrates that our strategy performs really well over a long period of time.

And by the way, Daniel I mentioned, part of this was actually a back test from 2010 to 2019. But after 2019, we started, running it. So you actually can see the trades that occurred from 2019, and the performance has been very, very strong for those simulated trades.

Now on another account of looking at our performance, every January I pick Top 10 stocks. And in 2023, when we picked our Top 10 stocks, looking at the Strong Buys then, the performance was amazing. It was up 73% versus the S&P 500, up 26% for 2023. But, Daniel, we did it again in January of 2024, and here's the performance for the Top 10 stocks for last year, it was up on average a whopping 125% versus the Mag 7, which was up 57%. And that's pretty outstanding in its own right, but it's quite amazing that our Top 10 stocks, so significantly outperformed the Mag 7. And, of course, the S&P 500 for the same period was up 25%.

When you look at this table, Daniel, you can actually see in the beginning of the year, we -- the orange line, which is our top Quant picks, they traded fairly well, January, February, March. But then the -- there was a rotation in the market. Early on in the year, investors were feeling really comfortable that inflation was going to sort of be tamed and that interest rates are going to be coming down. But after April, we actually had a couple economic indicators come out that really scared the market. So between April and August, the market was incredibly volatile. You probably remember in July and August, we had immense volatility. And when that volatility occurred, that was a perfect example of how the market in a defensive manner rotated into the Magnificent 7 stocks.

So you can actually see on this chart, where the Magnificent 7 started to trade really well, and our Top 10 stocks sort of plateaued, and that actually started coming down, quite significantly in July and August.
